schools.summary: The heading of the statutes to be kept in the land: 'these are the statutes and judgments, which ye shall observe to do in the land, which the LORD God of thy fathers giveth thee to possess it, all the days that ye live upon the earth' — Moses heads the body of statutes the people were to keep in the land (the these are the statutes and judgments, which ye shall observe to do, the statutes set forth, the in the land, which the LORD God of thy fathers giveth thee to possess it, the land of inheritance, the all the days that ye live upon the earth, the perpetual obligation, the figure, the heading of the laws of worship and life in the land, binding all their days, the truth that the statutes were given to be observed in the land, the rule of the people's life, the figure of the law set before the people for their walk, the statutes to be kept always, the obligation that runs all their days, the law of the land of promise, the introduction to the laws of worship that follow, vv.2-3); the statutes to be kept in the land (the heading) — the heading (the statutes); 'all the days that ye live upon the earth' (the perpetual obligation, the law always binding, the figure, the rule of the people's life, the providence, the heading, the destroying of the idols to follow, vv.2-3) — Henry, Gill.

themes: These are the statutes and judgments, which ye shall observe to do in the land, which the LORD God of thy fathers giveth thee to possess it, all the days that ye live upon the earth
the heading of the statutes to be kept in the land
Moses heading the body of statutes the people were to keep all their days
the rule of their life in the land of promise

schools.summary: The command to destroy all the places of idolatry: 'ye shall utterly destroy all the places, wherein the nations which ye shall possess served their gods, upon the high mountains, and upon the hills, and under every green tree' — Israel was to destroy utterly every site of the nations' idolatry (the ye shall utterly destroy all the places, the total destruction commanded, the wherein the nations which ye shall possess served their gods, the sites of idolatry, the upon the high mountains, and upon the hills, and under every green tree, the high places and groves, the figure, the utter destruction of the places of idolatry, that no trace remain, the truth that the worship of the true God required the rooting out of all false worship, the figure of the zeal against idolatry, the high places and groves to be destroyed, 'ye shall destroy their altars, break their images,' Exodus 34:13, the thoroughness of the purge, every site of idolatry removed, the command that the land be cleansed of false worship before the true be set up); the command to destroy the sites of idolatry (the purge, Exodus 34:13) — the destruction (the idol-places); 'upon the high mountains, and upon the hills, and under every green tree' (the high places and groves destroyed, the thorough purge, the figure, the zeal against idolatry, the providence, the destruction, the breaking of the altars to follow, vv.3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Ye shall utterly destroy all the places, wherein the nations which ye shall possess served their gods, upon the high mountains, and upon the hills, and under every green tree
the command to destroy all the places of idolatry
Israel to destroy every site of the nations' idolatry, the high places and groves
that the land be cleansed of false worship before the true be set up (Exodus 34:13)

schools.summary: The command to overthrow the altars and destroy the idols and their names: 'and ye shall overthrow their altars, and break their pillars, and burn their groves with fire; and ye shall hew down the graven images of their gods, and destroy the names of them out of that place' — every instrument of idolatry, even the names, was to be destroyed (the ye shall overthrow their altars, and break their pillars, the altars and pillars destroyed, the and burn their groves with fire; and ye shall hew down the graven images, the groves and images destroyed, the and destroy the names of them out of that place, the very names blotted out, the figure, the thoroughness of the destruction, even the names of the idols to be wiped out, the truth that idolatry was to be rooted out root and branch, nothing of it left, the figure of the zeal that destroys not only the idols but their memory, 'make no mention of the names of other gods,' Exodus 23:13, the command to blot out the names, that idolatry be utterly forgotten, the contrast with the place where God would put his name, vv.5); the altars, pillars, and groves destroyed (the purge, Exodus 23:13) — the destruction (the instruments of idolatry); 'destroy the names of them out of that place' (the very names blotted out, the memory of idolatry wiped, Exodus 23:13, the figure, idolatry rooted out root and branch, the providence, the destruction, the place God chooses to follow, vv.4-5) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And ye shall overthrow their altars, and break their pillars, and burn their groves with fire; and ye shall hew down the graven images of their gods, and destroy the names of them out of that place
the command to overthrow the altars and destroy the idols and their names
every instrument of idolatry destroyed, even the names blotted out
that idolatry be utterly forgotten and the land cleansed (Exodus 23:13)

schools.summary: That God is not to be worshipped as the idols were: 'ye shall not do so unto the LORD your God' — Israel was not to worship God in the scattered, self-chosen way the nations worshipped their idols (the ye shall not do so, the prohibition, the unto the LORD your God, the worship of the true God, the figure, the contrast between the nations' worship at every high place and the worship of God at the one place he would choose, the truth that the worship of God is not to follow the manner of the heathen, but his own appointment, the figure of the purity of God's worship, not to be conducted after the will of man or the way of the nations, the prohibition that prepares for the one chosen place, vv.5, the regulative truth that God is to be worshipped as he appoints, not as men devise, 'in vain they do worship me,' Matthew 15:9); God not to be worshipped as the idols were (the contrast) — the prohibition (not so); 'unto the LORD your God' (the worship of God not after the heathen manner, the regulative truth, Matthew 15:9, the figure, the purity of God's worship, the providence, the prohibition, the one chosen place to follow, vv.5) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Ye shall not do so unto the LORD your God
that God is not to be worshipped as the idols were
Israel not to worship God in the scattered, self-chosen way of the nations, but as he appoints
the purity of his worship against the heathen manner (Matthew 15:9)

schools.summary: The one place which the LORD would choose to put his name: 'but unto the place which the LORD your God shall choose out of all your tribes to put his name there, even unto his habitation shall ye seek, and thither thou shalt come' — the central sanctuary — 'unto the place which the LORD your God shall choose out of all your tribes to put his name there, even unto his habitation shall ye seek, and thither thou shalt come': God would choose one place, out of all the tribes, to set his name and be worshipped (the witnesses noting the significance — the one appointed place of worship, against the many high places of the nations, God himself choosing where his name should dwell, the tabernacle and afterward the temple at Jerusalem, the centre of the nation's worship, the lesson — the unity of God's worship, one God, one altar, one place, that the people not scatter into self-chosen worship, the place God chooses, not man, the seat of his name and presence, the gathering of the tribes to one sanctuary the bond of their unity in worship, the truth that God appoints the place and manner of his worship, 'in all places where I record my name I will come unto thee,' Exodus 20:24, the figure — the place where God puts his name a figure of Christ, in whom God's name dwells and to whom his people gather, 'in him should all fulness dwell,' Colossians 1:19, 'where two or three are gathered together in my name,' Matthew 18:20, the figure of the one way of access to God, the chosen place fulfilled in Christ the true sanctuary, 'neither in this mountain, nor yet at Jerusalem... the true worshippers shall worship the Father in spirit and in truth,' John 4:21-23, the truth that worship is acceptable only at God's appointment, the chosen place the type of the one Mediator, the figure of the unity of the church gathered to the one name, the figure of the central sanctuary, the one place of God's name, fulfilled in Christ); God choosing one place to put his name, the central sanctuary against the high places (the one appointed seat of worship, Exodus 20:24) — the place (God chooses); 'even unto his habitation shall ye seek, and thither thou shalt come' (the people to seek God's habitation and come thither, the unity of worship at the one place, the gathering to the one sanctuary, John 4:21-23) — the figure (Christ the true sanctuary, the one way of access, the providence, the chosen place, the offerings brought thither to follow, vv.6-7) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'the place which the LORD shall choose to put his name there' signify?

sides.position: The central sanctuary, the one appointed seat of God's name against the nations' high places - the unity of God's worship, one God and one altar (Exodus 20:24), a figure of Christ in whom God's name dwells and to whom his people gather (Colossians 1:19; Matthew 18:20), fulfilled in the worship in spirit and truth bound neither to mountain nor Jerusalem but to the one Mediator (John 4:21-23)

themes: But unto the place which the LORD your God shall choose out of all your tribes to put his name there, even unto his habitation shall ye seek, and thither thou shalt come
the one place which the LORD would choose to put his name (the crux of the central sanctuary and the unity of God's worship)
the one appointed seat of God's name against the scattered high places, the unity of worship, one God and one altar
a figure of Christ in whom God's name dwells and to whom his people gather, fulfilled in worship in spirit and truth (Exodus 20:24; Colossians 1:19; John 4:21-23)

schools.summary: The offerings to be brought to the chosen place: 'and thither ye shall bring your burnt offerings, and your sacrifices, and your tithes, and heave offerings of your hand, and your vows, and your freewill offerings, and the firstlings of your herds and of your flocks' — all the offerings were to be brought to the one chosen place (the thither ye shall bring your burnt offerings, and your sacrifices, the sacrifices, the and your tithes, and heave offerings of your hand, the tithes and heave offerings, the and your vows, and your freewill offerings, and the firstlings, the vows, freewill offerings, and firstlings, the figure, all the offerings gathered to the one place, the unity of worship in the place God chose, the truth that every kind of offering was to be brought to the central sanctuary, not offered at will in scattered places, the figure of the worship gathered to one altar, the tithes and sacrifices brought to God's appointed seat, the offerings of the people centred on the one place, the bond of their worship, the gifts brought to the chosen place that God's worship be one, vv.5); the sacrifices and tithes brought to the chosen place (the offerings) — the offerings (brought thither); 'your vows, and your freewill offerings, and the firstlings' (the vows and firstlings brought to the one place, the unity of worship, the figure, the offerings centred on one altar, the providence, the offerings, the joy before God to follow, vv.7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And thither ye shall bring your burnt offerings, and your sacrifices, and your tithes, and heave offerings of your hand, and your vows, and your freewill offerings, and the firstlings of your herds and of your flocks
the offerings to be brought to the chosen place
all the offerings brought to the one chosen place, the worship of the people gathered to God's appointed seat
the bond of their unity in worship

schools.summary: The eating and rejoicing before God at the chosen place: 'and there ye shall eat before the LORD your God, and ye shall rejoice in all that ye put your hand unto, ye and your households, wherein the LORD thy God hath blessed thee' — at the chosen place the people would eat their offerings and rejoice before God (the there ye shall eat before the LORD your God, the eating before God, the and ye shall rejoice in all that ye put your hand unto, the joy in their labour, the ye and your households, wherein the LORD thy God hath blessed thee, the household joy in God's blessing, the figure, the worship that is joyful feasting before God, the people rejoicing in his blessing, the truth that God's worship is not only sacrifice but joy, the feast before the LORD, the figure of the joy of communion with God, the people eating and rejoicing in his presence, 'rejoice before the LORD your God,' Deuteronomy 12:12, the gladness that befits the worship of God, the household feasting in his blessing, the joy of the redeemed before God, the foretaste of the eternal feast); the eating of the offerings before God (the feast) — the eating (before God); 'ye shall rejoice in all that ye put your hand unto' (the joy in God's blessing, the gladness of worship, Deuteronomy 12:12, the figure, the joy of communion with God, the providence, the joy, the warning against scattered worship to follow, vv.8) — Henry, Gill.

themes: And there ye shall eat before the LORD your God, and ye shall rejoice in all that ye put your hand unto, ye and your households, wherein the LORD thy God hath blessed thee
the eating and rejoicing before God at the chosen place
the people eating their offerings and rejoicing before God, the worship that is joyful feasting in his blessing
the gladness that befits communion with God (Deuteronomy 12:12)

schools.summary: The warning against every man worshipping as is right in his own eyes: 'ye shall not do after all the things that we do here this day, every man whatsoever is right in his own eyes' — the rebuke of self-will — 'ye shall not do after all the things that we do here this day, every man whatsoever is right in his own eyes': in the land they were not to worship as each thought right, but at the one place by God's appointment (the witnesses noting the significance — the prohibition of self-chosen, self-willed worship, in the wilderness, with no settled place, there had been some liberty, but in the land they must worship only at the chosen place, not every man as he pleased, the lesson — that worship is to be regulated by God's command, not by man's private judgment, the danger of every man doing what is right in his own eyes, the very mark of disorder and apostasy, 'every man did that which was right in his own eyes,' Judges 17:6; 21:25, the truth that God's worship is not left to human invention but bound to his appointment, will-worship condemned, 'in vain they do worship me, teaching for doctrines the commandments of men,' Matthew 15:9, the figure — the regulative principle of worship, that God is to be served as he commands and not as men devise, 'which I commanded them not, neither came it into my heart,' Jeremiah 7:31, the truth that self-willed worship, however sincere, is rejected, the worship of God to follow his word, the figure of the order and unity of true worship against the chaos of private religion, the warning against the autonomy that makes each man his own authority in worship, the figure of the purity of worship by God's appointment against every man's own eyes); the wilderness liberty would cease, the worship to be at the one place in the land (the change to the appointed worship) — the warning (not as here); 'every man whatsoever is right in his own eyes' (the rebuke of self-chosen worship, every man his own authority, the very mark of apostasy, the will-worship condemned, Judges 17:6; Matthew 15:9) — the figure (the regulative principle, worship by God's command, the providence, the warning, the rest in the land to follow, vv.9-10) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Wesley.

debates.question: What does 'every man whatsoever is right in his own eyes' warn against?

sides.position: Self-chosen, self-willed worship - worship is to be regulated by God's command, not man's private judgment; every man doing what is right in his own eyes is the mark of apostasy (Judges 17:6), will-worship condemned (Matthew 15:9), the worship of God to follow his appointment and not man's invention (Jeremiah 7:31)

themes: Ye shall not do after all the things that we do here this day, every man whatsoever is right in his own eyes
the warning against every man worshipping as is right in his own eyes (the crux of the regulative purity of worship against self-will)
the prohibition of self-chosen worship, bound instead to God's appointment, the danger of every man doing what is right in his own eyes the mark of apostasy
will-worship condemned, the worship of God to follow his command and not man's invention (Judges 17:6; Matthew 15:9; Jeremiah 7:31)

schools.summary: That they had not yet come to the rest and inheritance: 'for ye are not as yet come to the rest and to the inheritance, which the LORD your God giveth you' — the people had not yet reached the settled rest of the land where the one place would be fixed (the for ye are not as yet come to the rest, the rest not yet reached, the and to the inheritance, which the LORD your God giveth you, the inheritance still future, the figure, the rest of the land still ahead, the settled worship not yet established, the truth that the full ordering of worship awaited the rest in the land, the figure of the rest of Canaan a type of the rest that remains for the people of God, 'there remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God,' Hebrews 4:9, the inheritance still future, the worship to be settled when the rest was come, the pilgrim state before the rest, the people not yet at home); the rest of the land not yet reached (the future rest, Hebrews 4:9) — the rest (not yet come); 'the inheritance, which the LORD your God giveth you' (the inheritance still future, the settled rest, Hebrews 4:9, the figure, Canaan a type of the rest that remains, the providence, the rest, the settled worship to follow, vv.10-11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: For ye are not as yet come to the rest and to the inheritance, which the LORD your God giveth you
that they had not yet come to the rest and inheritance
the people not yet at the settled rest of the land where the one place would be fixed
a type of the rest that remains for the people of God (Hebrews 4:9)

schools.summary: The rest in the land when their enemies were subdued: 'but when ye go over Jordan, and dwell in the land which the LORD your God giveth you to inherit, and when he giveth you rest from all your enemies round about, so that ye dwell in safety' — when settled in the land and given rest from their enemies, the one place would be appointed (the but when ye go over Jordan, and dwell in the land, the settlement in the land, the and when he giveth you rest from all your enemies round about, the rest from enemies, the so that ye dwell in safety, the safe dwelling, the figure, the rest and safety of the land the setting for the settled worship, the truth that God would give rest from enemies, the people dwelling safely, the time for the fixed sanctuary, the figure of the rest and peace that God gives his people, the safety in which his worship is established, the rest from enemies the gift of God, 'the LORD gave them rest round about,' Joshua 21:44, the settled state in which the one place would be chosen, vv.11); the settlement in the land (the inheritance) — the settlement (in the land); 'he giveth you rest from all your enemies round about' (the rest and safety from enemies, God's gift, Joshua 21:44, the figure, the peace in which worship is settled, the providence, the rest, the one place chosen to follow, vv.11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: But when ye go over Jordan, and dwell in the land which the LORD your God giveth you to inherit, and when he giveth you rest from all your enemies round about, so that ye dwell in safety
the rest in the land when their enemies were subdued
the people settled in the land and given rest from their enemies
the safe dwelling in which the one place of worship would be chosen (Joshua 21:44)

schools.summary: The one place God would choose for his name and the offerings: 'then there shall be a place which the LORD your God shall choose to cause his name to dwell there; thither shall ye bring all that I command you; your burnt offerings, and your sacrifices, your tithes, and the heave offering of your hand, and all your choice vows which ye vow unto the LORD' — God would choose one place for his name, to which all the offerings were to be brought (the then there shall be a place which the LORD your God shall choose to cause his name to dwell there, the chosen place, the thither shall ye bring all that I command you; your burnt offerings, and your sacrifices, the offerings brought, the your tithes... and all your choice vows which ye vow unto the LORD, the tithes and vows, the figure, the one place for God's name, the offerings all brought thither, the unity of worship, the truth that God would settle his name in one place, the centre of worship, the figure of the chosen place where God's name dwells, fulfilled in Christ in whom God dwells, vv.5, the offerings gathered to the one place, the worship of the people centred, the place of God's name the seat of his worship, the bond of the nation); the chosen place for God's name (the central sanctuary) — the place (for his name); 'thither shall ye bring all that I command you' (the offerings brought to the one place, the unity of worship, the figure, the worship centred on God's name, the providence, the place, the joy before God to follow, vv.12) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Then there shall be a place which the LORD your God shall choose to cause his name to dwell there; thither shall ye bring all that I command you; your burnt offerings, and your sacrifices, your tithes, and the heave offering of your hand, and all your choice vows which ye vow unto the LORD
the one place God would choose for his name and the offerings
God choosing one place for his name, to which all the offerings were brought
the worship of the people centred and the bond of the nation

schools.summary: The joy before God shared by all, including the Levite: 'and ye shall rejoice before the LORD your God, ye, and your sons, and your daughters, and your menservants, and your maidservants, and the Levite that is within your gates; forasmuch as he hath no part nor inheritance with you' — all the household, and the landless Levite, were to rejoice before God (the ye shall rejoice before the LORD your God, the joy before God, the ye, and your sons, and your daughters, and your menservants, and your maidservants, the whole household, the and the Levite that is within your gates; forasmuch as he hath no part nor inheritance, the Levite included, having no land, the figure, the joy of worship shared by all, the household and the landless Levite alike, the truth that the worship of God is a shared joy, none excluded, the Levite cared for, the figure of the inclusive joy of God's people, the servants and the Levite rejoicing together, 'rejoice in the LORD alway,' Philippians 4:4, the care for the Levite who had no inheritance, the worship that provides for him, the gladness of the whole community before God); the whole household rejoicing before God (the shared joy, Philippians 4:4) — the joy (of all); 'the Levite that is within your gates; forasmuch as he hath no part nor inheritance' (the landless Levite included, the care for him, the figure, the inclusive joy of worship, the providence, the joy, the warning not to offer in every place to follow, vv.13) — Henry, Gill.

themes: And ye shall rejoice before the LORD your God, ye, and your sons, and your daughters, and your menservants, and your maidservants, and the Levite that is within your gates; forasmuch as he hath no part nor inheritance with you
the joy before God shared by all, including the Levite
the whole household and the landless Levite rejoicing together before God
the shared gladness of worship that excludes none and cares for the Levite (Philippians 4:4)

schools.summary: The warning not to offer burnt offerings in any place at will: 'take heed to thyself that thou offer not thy burnt offerings in every place that thou seest' — they were not to sacrifice wherever they pleased, but only at the chosen place (the take heed to thyself, the solemn warning, the that thou offer not thy burnt offerings in every place that thou seest, the prohibition of sacrifice at will, the figure, the warning against scattered, self-chosen sacrifice, the worship bound to the one place, the truth that the people were not to offer wherever it seemed good to them, but only where God appointed, the figure of the restraint of worship to God's appointment, against the high places of the heathen, the prohibition that guards the unity and purity of worship, no offering in every place, the worship confined to the chosen place, that it not scatter into idolatry, vv.14); the solemn warning against offering at will (the restraint) — the warning (take heed); 'that thou offer not thy burnt offerings in every place that thou seest' (the prohibition of sacrifice at will, the worship bound to one place, the figure, the restraint of worship to God's appointment, the providence, the warning, the offering only at the chosen place to follow, vv.14) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Take heed to thyself that thou offer not thy burnt offerings in every place that thou seest
the warning not to offer burnt offerings in any place at will
the people not to sacrifice wherever they pleased but only at the chosen place
the worship bound to God's appointment that it not scatter into idolatry

schools.summary: That sacrifice was to be offered only at the place God chooses: 'but in the place which the LORD shall choose in one of thy tribes, there thou shalt offer thy burnt offerings, and there thou shalt do all that I command thee' — sacrifice and all commanded worship were to be at the one chosen place (the but in the place which the LORD shall choose in one of thy tribes, the chosen place, the there thou shalt offer thy burnt offerings, the sacrifice there, the and there thou shalt do all that I command thee, all worship there, the figure, the worship confined to the chosen place, all the commanded service there, the truth that sacrifice and worship were bound to God's appointed seat, not scattered, the figure of the unity of worship at the one place, all done as God commands, the place of God's choice the only lawful altar, the worship centred, the obedience that worships where and as God appoints, vv.13); the sacrifice at the one chosen place (the appointed altar) — the place (God chooses); 'there thou shalt do all that I command thee' (all worship at the one place, the unity and obedience, the figure, the worship centred and obedient, the providence, the place, the eating of common flesh allowed to follow, vv.15) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: But in the place which the LORD shall choose in one of thy tribes, there thou shalt offer thy burnt offerings, and there thou shalt do all that I command thee
that sacrifice was to be offered only at the place God chooses
sacrifice and all commanded worship at the one chosen place
the worship centred on God's appointed seat and done as he commands

schools.summary: The liberty to eat common flesh in all their gates: 'notwithstanding thou mayest kill and eat flesh in all thy gates, whatsoever thy soul lusteth after, according to the blessing of the LORD thy God which he hath given thee: the unclean and the clean may eat thereof, as of the roebuck, and as of the hart' — apart from the sacrifices, they might kill and eat common flesh anywhere, clean and unclean alike (the notwithstanding thou mayest kill and eat flesh in all thy gates, the liberty to eat flesh at home, the whatsoever thy soul lusteth after, according to the blessing of the LORD, the freedom according to God's blessing, the the unclean and the clean may eat thereof, as of the roebuck, and as of the hart, common flesh, like game, the figure, the distinction between sacrifice and common food, the liberty to eat flesh freely at home, the truth that while sacrifice was confined to the one place, ordinary eating of flesh was free, the figure of the liberty God grants in things indifferent, the common meal unrestricted, the freedom to enjoy God's blessing in food, the clean and unclean eating common flesh, the gracious provision that the people enjoy flesh as game, apart from the altar); the freedom to eat common flesh at home (the liberty) — the liberty (eat flesh in all thy gates); 'the unclean and the clean may eat thereof, as of the roebuck, and as of the hart' (common flesh eaten like game, the liberty in food, the figure, the freedom in things indifferent, the providence, the liberty, the prohibition of eating blood to follow, vv.16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Notwithstanding thou mayest kill and eat flesh in all thy gates, whatsoever thy soul lusteth after, according to the blessing of the LORD thy God which he hath given thee: the unclean and the clean may eat thereof, as of the roebuck, and as of the hart
the liberty to eat common flesh in all their gates
the people free to kill and eat common flesh anywhere, apart from the sacrifices
the gracious liberty to enjoy God's blessing in food

schools.summary: The prohibition of eating blood, to be poured on the earth: 'only ye shall not eat the blood; ye shall pour it upon the earth as water' — though they might eat common flesh freely, they must not eat the blood, but pour it out (the only ye shall not eat the blood, the prohibition of blood, the ye shall pour it upon the earth as water, the blood poured out, the figure, the abiding prohibition of eating blood, even with common flesh, the truth that the blood, the life, was reserved and not to be eaten, poured out on the earth, the figure of the sacredness of blood, the life belonging to God, 'the life of the flesh is in the blood,' Leviticus 17:11, the prohibition that points to the value of life and the blood of atonement, the blood poured out as water, not consumed, the reverence for the life God gave, the law of blood that runs through Scripture, fulfilled in the blood of Christ, vv.23); the prohibition of eating blood (the life reserved, Leviticus 17:11) — the prohibition (not the blood); 'ye shall pour it upon the earth as water' (the blood poured out, not eaten, the reverence for life, Leviticus 17:11, the figure, the sacredness of blood, the life God's, the providence, the prohibition, the eating only at the chosen place to follow, vv.17-18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Only ye shall not eat the blood; ye shall pour it upon the earth as water
the prohibition of eating blood, to be poured on the earth
the people not to eat the blood even with common flesh, but pour it out, the life reserved as belonging to God
the reverence for the blood fulfilled in the blood of Christ (Leviticus 17:11)

schools.summary: That the holy things were not to be eaten at home but at the chosen place: 'thou mayest not eat within thy gates the tithe of thy corn, or of thy wine, or of thy oil, or the firstlings of thy herds or of thy flock, nor any of thy vows which thou vowest, nor thy freewill offerings, or heave offering of thine hand' — the tithes, firstlings, vows, and offerings were not to be eaten at home (the thou mayest not eat within thy gates the tithe of thy corn, or of thy wine, or of thy oil, the tithes not eaten at home, the or the firstlings of thy herds or of thy flock, the firstlings, the nor any of thy vows which thou vowest, nor thy freewill offerings, the vows and offerings, the figure, the holy things reserved for the chosen place, not the common table, the truth that the offerings devoted to God were to be eaten before him, not at home, the figure of the distinction between the holy and the common, the offerings kept for the sanctuary, the holy things to be brought to the one place, not consumed at will, the reverence due to what is devoted to God, vv.18); the tithes and firstlings not eaten at home (the holy things reserved) — the prohibition (not at home); 'nor any of thy vows... nor thy freewill offerings' (the vows and offerings reserved for the chosen place, the holy things, the figure, the distinction of holy and common, the providence, the prohibition, the eating at the chosen place to follow, vv.18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Thou mayest not eat within thy gates the tithe of thy corn, or of thy wine, or of thy oil, or the firstlings of thy herds or of thy flock, nor any of thy vows which thou vowest, nor thy freewill offerings, or heave offering of thine hand
that the holy things were not to be eaten at home but at the chosen place
the tithes, firstlings, vows, and offerings reserved for the chosen place, not the common table
the reverence due to what is devoted to God

schools.summary: The eating of the holy things before God at the chosen place with joy: 'but thou must eat them before the LORD thy God in the place which the LORD thy God shall choose, thou, and thy son, and thy daughter, and thy manservant, and thy maidservant, and the Levite that is within thy gates: and thou shalt rejoice before the LORD thy God in all that thou puttest thine hands unto' — the holy things were to be eaten before God at the chosen place, with the household and the Levite, in joy (the but thou must eat them before the LORD thy God in the place which the LORD thy God shall choose, the eating at the chosen place, the thou, and thy son, and thy daughter... and the Levite that is within thy gates, the household and Levite, the and thou shalt rejoice before the LORD thy God in all that thou puttest thine hands unto, the joy before God, the figure, the holy things eaten before God in joy, the worship of the one place a feast, the truth that the offerings were to be enjoyed before God, the worship glad and shared, the figure of the joy of communion at the chosen place, the household and Levite rejoicing, the feast before the LORD, the offerings eaten in his presence with gladness, vv.12, the worship that is joyful fellowship before God); the holy things eaten before God at the chosen place (the feast) — the eating (before God); 'thou shalt rejoice before the LORD thy God' (the joy in worship, the gladness of the feast, Deuteronomy 12:12, the figure, the joy of communion before God, the providence, the eating, the care for the Levite to follow, vv.19) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: But thou must eat them before the LORD thy God in the place which the LORD thy God shall choose, thou, and thy son, and thy daughter, and thy manservant, and thy maidservant, and the Levite that is within thy gates: and thou shalt rejoice before the LORD thy God in all that thou puttest thine hands unto
the eating of the holy things before God at the chosen place with joy
the holy things eaten before God at the chosen place with the household and Levite
the worship that is joyful fellowship before God (Deuteronomy 12:12)

schools.summary: The charge not to forsake the Levite: 'take heed to thyself that thou forsake not the Levite as long as thou livest upon the earth' — the people were charged never to neglect the Levite, who had no inheritance (the take heed to thyself, the solemn charge, the that thou forsake not the Levite, the Levite not to be neglected, the as long as thou livest upon the earth, the perpetual duty, the figure, the abiding care for the Levite, who served at the sanctuary and had no land, the truth that those who minister in holy things are to be provided for, never forsaken, the figure of the support due to the ministers of God, 'they which minister about holy things live of the things of the temple,' 1 Corinthians 9:13, the perpetual charge to care for the Levite, the worship that provides for its servants, the duty of God's people to their ministers, all their days); the solemn charge to care for the Levite (the duty) — the charge (take heed); 'that thou forsake not the Levite' (the Levite never to be neglected, the support of the ministers, 1 Corinthians 9:13, the figure, the care due to God's servants, the providence, the charge, the liberty to eat flesh enlarged to follow, vv.20-22) — Henry, Gill.

themes: Take heed to thyself that thou forsake not the Levite as long as thou livest upon the earth
the charge not to forsake the Levite
the people charged never to neglect the Levite who had no inheritance
the abiding duty to provide for those who minister in holy things (1 Corinthians 9:13)

schools.summary: The liberty to eat flesh when the border is enlarged: 'when the LORD thy God shall enlarge thy border, as he hath promised thee, and thou shalt say, I will eat flesh, because thy soul longeth to eat flesh; thou mayest eat flesh, whatsoever thy soul lusteth after' — when settled and prospered, they might eat flesh freely as they desired (the when the LORD thy God shall enlarge thy border, as he hath promised thee, the enlarged land, the and thou shalt say, I will eat flesh, because thy soul longeth to eat flesh, the desire for flesh, the thou mayest eat flesh, whatsoever thy soul lusteth after, the liberty granted, the figure, the gracious liberty to eat flesh in the prospered land, the desire allowed, the truth that God grants his people the enjoyment of his blessings, the flesh eaten freely, the figure of the liberty in things indifferent, the common food enjoyed, the freedom that God gives in the enlarged land, the appetite satisfied with thanksgiving, the provision for the people's desire, the flesh eaten in all their gates, vv.15); the desire for flesh in the enlarged land (the liberty) — the desire (to eat flesh); 'thou mayest eat flesh, whatsoever thy soul lusteth after' (the freedom to eat flesh granted, the liberty in food, the figure, the enjoyment of God's blessings, the providence, the liberty, the distance from the chosen place allowing home slaughter to follow, vv.21) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: When the LORD thy God shall enlarge thy border, as he hath promised thee, and thou shalt say, I will eat flesh, because thy soul longeth to eat flesh; thou mayest eat flesh, whatsoever thy soul lusteth after
the liberty to eat flesh when the border is enlarged
the people, settled and prospered, free to eat flesh as they desired
the gracious liberty to enjoy God's blessings

schools.summary: That home slaughter was allowed when far from the chosen place: 'if the place which the LORD thy God hath chosen to put his name there be too far from thee, then thou shalt kill of thy herd and of thy flock, which the LORD hath given thee, as I have commanded thee, and thou shalt eat in thy gates whatsoever thy soul lusteth after' — when too far from the sanctuary, they might slaughter and eat common flesh at home (the if the place which the LORD thy God hath chosen to put his name there be too far from thee, the distance from the sanctuary, the then thou shalt kill of thy herd and of thy flock... as I have commanded thee, the home slaughter allowed, the and thou shalt eat in thy gates whatsoever thy soul lusteth after, the eating at home, the figure, the gracious allowance for those far from the sanctuary, the common flesh eaten at home, the truth that God's law made provision for distance, the people not burdened to bring all flesh to the one place, the figure of the gracious accommodation of the law to the people's circumstances, the common meal free, the distinction between the holy offering at the place and common flesh at home, vv.15, the liberty granted for the distant, the flesh eaten in their gates); the home slaughter allowed for those far off (the accommodation) — the allowance (kill at home); 'thou shalt eat in thy gates whatsoever thy soul lusteth after' (the common flesh eaten at home, the liberty for the distant, the figure, the gracious accommodation of the law, the providence, the allowance, the blood still forbidden to follow, vv.22-23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: If the place which the LORD thy God hath chosen to put his name there be too far from thee, then thou shalt kill of thy herd and of thy flock, which the LORD hath given thee, as I have commanded thee, and thou shalt eat in thy gates whatsoever thy soul lusteth after
that home slaughter was allowed when far from the chosen place
the people far from the sanctuary allowed to slaughter and eat common flesh at home
the law's gracious provision for distance

schools.summary: That common flesh was eaten like game, clean and unclean alike: 'even as the roebuck and the hart is eaten, so thou shalt eat them: the unclean and the clean shall eat of them alike' — common flesh was eaten like game, by clean and unclean together (the even as the roebuck and the hart is eaten, like the eating of game, the so thou shalt eat them, the common flesh eaten, the the unclean and the clean shall eat of them alike, no distinction of persons, the figure, the common flesh eaten freely, like game, by all alike, the truth that ordinary food was free to all, the ceremonial distinctions not applying, the figure of the liberty in common things, the clean and unclean eating together, the distinction between the holy offering, restricted, and the common meal, free, the gracious liberty in food, no ceremonial bar, vv.15); the common flesh eaten like game (the liberty) — the eating (like game); 'the unclean and the clean shall eat of them alike' (no distinction in common food, the liberty for all, the figure, the freedom in common things, the providence, the liberty, the blood forbidden to follow, vv.23) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Even as the roebuck and the hart is eaten, so thou shalt eat them: the unclean and the clean shall eat of them alike
that common flesh was eaten like game, clean and unclean alike
the common flesh eaten freely like game, by clean and unclean together
the gracious liberty in food without ceremonial bar

schools.summary: The prohibition of eating blood, for the blood is the life: 'only be sure that thou eat not the blood: for the blood is the life; and thou mayest not eat the life with the flesh' — the life in the blood — 'only be sure that thou eat not the blood: for the blood is the life; and thou mayest not eat the life with the flesh': the blood was not to be eaten, because it is the life, which belongs to God (the witnesses noting the significance — the sacredness of the blood as the seat of life, the life is in the blood, and the life belongs to God, so the blood is not to be eaten but reserved, the lesson — the reverence for life and the reservation of the blood to God, the blood, as the life, set apart from common use, 'the life of the flesh is in the blood... it is the blood that maketh an atonement for the soul,' Leviticus 17:11; 14, the truth that the blood, the life, is sacred, given by God for atonement, not to be consumed as food, the figure — the blood that is the life points to the blood of atonement, the life given for the soul, fulfilled in the blood of Christ, 'the blood of Jesus Christ his Son cleanseth us from all sin,' 1 John 1:7, 'without shedding of blood is no remission,' Hebrews 9:22, the figure of the precious blood, the life poured out for redemption, 'ye were... redeemed... with the precious blood of Christ,' 1 Peter 1:18-19, the truth that the reverence for blood under the law guards the great doctrine of atonement by blood, the figure of the life in the blood reserved to God, the type of the life of Christ given for sinners, the figure of the blood as the life, sacred and reserved, pointing to the blood of atonement); the firm prohibition of eating blood, the blood reserved (the abiding law, Leviticus 17:11) — the prohibition (not the blood); 'for the blood is the life; and thou mayest not eat the life with the flesh' (the blood is the life, belonging to God, not to be eaten, the seat of life reserved, the life in the blood, the atonement, Leviticus 17:11; 1 John 1:7) — the figure (the blood of atonement, fulfilled in Christ's blood, the providence, the prohibition, the blood poured out to follow, vv.24) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Why was the blood not to be eaten?

sides.position: Because the blood is the life, which belongs to God - the seat of life reserved to God and not to be consumed, given by God for atonement (Leviticus 17:11), a figure of the blood of atonement and the precious blood of Christ poured out for redemption (1 John 1:7; Hebrews 9:22; 1 Peter 1:18-19), the law of blood that guards the doctrine of atonement

themes: Only be sure that thou eat not the blood: for the blood is the life; and thou mayest not eat the life with the flesh
the prohibition of eating blood, for the blood is the life (the crux of the sacredness of blood as the life and its reservation to God)
the blood, the seat of life, reserved to God and not to be eaten, given for atonement
a figure of the precious blood of Christ poured out for the redemption of sinners (Leviticus 17:11; 1 John 1:7; Hebrews 9:22; 1 Peter 1:18-19)

schools.summary: The blood to be poured out, not eaten: 'thou shalt not eat it; thou shalt pour it upon the earth as water' — the blood was to be poured out on the ground, not consumed (the thou shalt not eat it, the blood not eaten, the thou shalt pour it upon the earth as water, the blood poured out, the figure, the blood poured out as common water, not eaten, the life returned to God, the truth that the blood, the life, was to be poured out, reserved from common use, the figure of the reverence for the blood, poured out and not consumed, the life given back, the repeated command that guards the sacredness of blood, vv.16, 23, the blood poured out a figure of the blood shed in sacrifice, the life rendered to God); the blood not to be eaten (the prohibition) — the prohibition (not eaten); 'thou shalt pour it upon the earth as water' (the blood poured out, not consumed, the reverence for the life, the figure, the life rendered to God, the providence, the pouring, the blessing on obedience to follow, vv.25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Thou shalt not eat it; thou shalt pour it upon the earth as water
the blood to be poured out, not eaten
the blood poured out on the ground and not consumed
the life reserved from common use and rendered to God

schools.summary: The blessing on obedience in keeping the blood-law: 'thou shalt not eat it; that it may go well with thee, and with thy children after thee, when thou shalt do that which is right in the sight of the LORD' — obedience in not eating the blood would bring blessing on them and their children (the thou shalt not eat it, the prohibition repeated, the that it may go well with thee, and with thy children after thee, the blessing on obedience, the when thou shalt do that which is right in the sight of the LORD, the doing of God's will, the figure, the blessing that follows obedience, even in the law of blood, the truth that keeping God's commands, in things great and small, brings well-being, the figure of the reward of obedience, the well-being of the obedient and their children, 'that it may be well with thee,' Ephesians 6:3, the doing of what is right in God's sight the way of blessing, the connexion of obedience and blessing, even in the ceremonial law); the blessing on keeping the blood-law (the well-being, Ephesians 6:3) — the blessing (it may go well); 'when thou shalt do that which is right in the sight of the LORD' (the doing of God's will, the obedience that brings blessing, the figure, the reward of obedience, the providence, the blessing, the holy things brought to the place to follow, vv.26) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Thou shalt not eat it; that it may go well with thee, and with thy children after thee, when thou shalt do that which is right in the sight of the LORD
the blessing on obedience in keeping the blood-law
obedience in not eating the blood bringing blessing on them and their children
the doing of what is right in God's sight the way of well-being (Ephesians 6:3)

schools.summary: That the holy things and vows were to be brought to the chosen place: 'only thy holy things which thou hast, and thy vows, thou shalt take, and go unto the place which the LORD shall choose' — the holy things and vows, unlike common flesh, were to be brought to the one place (the only thy holy things which thou hast, and thy vows, the holy things and vows, the thou shalt take, and go unto the place which the LORD shall choose, brought to the chosen place, the figure, the holy things reserved for the sanctuary, not eaten at home like common flesh, the truth that what is devoted to God belongs to his appointed place, the figure of the distinction between the holy and the common, the offerings brought to the one altar, the reverence for the devoted things, taken to the chosen place, the worship centred, the holy things at the sanctuary, vv.17-18); the holy things and vows reserved (the devoted things) — the holy things (and vows); 'thou shalt take, and go unto the place which the LORD shall choose' (the holy things brought to the chosen place, the worship centred, the figure, the devoted things at the sanctuary, the providence, the holy things, the offering of the sacrifices to follow, vv.27) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Only thy holy things which thou hast, and thy vows, thou shalt take, and go unto the place which the LORD shall choose
that the holy things and vows were to be brought to the chosen place
the holy things and vows brought to the one place, not eaten at home like common flesh
the worship centred on God's appointed seat

schools.summary: The burnt offerings offered and the blood poured on the altar: 'and thou shalt offer thy burnt offerings, the flesh and the blood, upon the altar of the LORD thy God: and the blood of thy sacrifices shall be poured out upon the altar of the LORD thy God, and thou shalt eat the flesh' — the burnt offerings were wholly offered, the blood poured on the altar, the flesh of peace offerings eaten (the and thou shalt offer thy burnt offerings, the flesh and the blood, upon the altar, the burnt offerings wholly offered, the and the blood of thy sacrifices shall be poured out upon the altar, the blood poured on the altar, the and thou shalt eat the flesh, the flesh of the peace offerings eaten, the figure, the right ordering of sacrifice at the altar, the blood poured to God, the flesh eaten, the truth that the blood, even in sacrifice, was poured on the altar, not eaten, the life given to God, the figure of the blood of sacrifice rendered to God on the altar, the type of the blood of atonement, the worship at the chosen place, the offerings rightly made, vv.23, the blood poured to God, the flesh of communion eaten); the burnt offerings wholly offered on the altar (the sacrifice) — the offering (burnt offerings); 'the blood... poured out upon the altar... and thou shalt eat the flesh' (the blood to God, the flesh eaten, the right sacrifice, vv.23, the figure, the blood of sacrifice rendered to God, the providence, the offering, the warning against the snare of the nations to follow, vv.28-30) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt offer thy burnt offerings, the flesh and the blood, upon the altar of the LORD thy God: and the blood of thy sacrifices shall be poured out upon the altar of the LORD thy God, and thou shalt eat the flesh
the burnt offerings offered and the blood poured on the altar
the burnt offerings wholly offered, the blood poured to God on the altar, the flesh of the peace offerings eaten
the worship rightly made at the chosen place

schools.summary: The call to obey all these words for lasting blessing: 'observe and hear all these words which I command thee, that it may go well with thee, and with thy children after thee for ever, when thou doest that which is good and right in the sight of the LORD thy God' — obedience to all God's commands would bring lasting blessing (the observe and hear all these words which I command thee, the call to obey all, the that it may go well with thee, and with thy children after thee for ever, the lasting blessing, the when thou doest that which is good and right in the sight of the LORD thy God, the doing of good and right, the figure, the blessing that follows full obedience, the well-being of the obedient and their children for ever, the truth that the keeping of all God's words is the way of lasting blessing, the figure of the reward of obedience, the good and right done before God, 'keep his statutes... that it may go well with thee,' Deuteronomy 4:40, the obedience that secures the blessing for the generations, the doing of good and right the way of well-being for ever); the call to obey all God's words for lasting blessing (the well-being, Deuteronomy 4:40) — the obedience (observe and hear); 'when thou doest that which is good and right in the sight of the LORD' (the doing of good and right, the obedience that blesses, the figure, the reward of obedience, the providence, the call, the warning against the snare of the nations to follow, vv.29-30) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Observe and hear all these words which I command thee, that it may go well with thee, and with thy children after thee for ever, when thou doest that which is good and right in the sight of the LORD thy God
the call to obey all these words for lasting blessing
obedience to all God's commands bringing lasting blessing on them and their children
the doing of good and right the way of well-being for ever (Deuteronomy 4:40)

schools.summary: The warning set after the nations are cut off and Israel dwells in the land: 'when the LORD thy God shall cut off the nations from before thee, whither thou goest to possess them, and thou succeedest them, and dwellest in their land' — after the nations are destroyed and Israel settled in their land, the warning follows (the when the LORD thy God shall cut off the nations from before thee, the nations destroyed, the whither thou goest to possess them, and thou succeedest them, Israel succeeding them, the and dwellest in their land, settled in the land, the figure, the setting of the warning, Israel settled where the nations had been, the truth that the very dwelling in the nations' land was the occasion of danger, the temptation to their idolatry, the figure of the snare that lies in the conquered land, the danger of imitating the nations' worship, the warning placed at the point of settlement, lest the people inquire after the nations' gods, vv.30, the danger of prosperity and possession, the temptation to apostasy); the nations destroyed before Israel (the conquest) — the setting (the nations cut off); 'thou succeedest them, and dwellest in their land' (Israel settled in the nations' land, the occasion of danger, the figure, the snare in the conquered land, the providence, the setting, the warning against inquiring after their gods to follow, vv.30) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: When the LORD thy God shall cut off the nations from before thee, whither thou goest to possess them, and thou succeedest them, and dwellest in their land
the warning set after the nations are cut off and Israel dwells in the land
Israel settled where the nations had been, the very dwelling in their land the occasion of temptation
the danger of imitating the nations' idolatry

schools.summary: The warning not to be snared by inquiring after the nations' gods: 'take heed to thyself that thou be not snared by following them, after that they be destroyed from before thee; and that thou enquire not after their gods, saying, How did these nations serve their gods? even so will I do likewise' — they were warned not to be ensnared into curiosity about and imitation of the nations' worship (the take heed to thyself that thou be not snared by following them, the warning against the snare, the and that thou enquire not after their gods, the curiosity forbidden, the saying, How did these nations serve their gods? even so will I do likewise, the temptation to imitate, the figure, the snare of curiosity about false worship, the temptation to imitate the nations, the truth that even inquiring after the manner of idolatry is a snare, the curiosity that leads to apostasy, the figure of the danger of dabbling with false religion, the inquiry that ensnares, the warning against importing the nations' worship into the service of God, 'learn not the way of the heathen,' Jeremiah 10:2, the snare that begins with curiosity and ends in idolatry); the warning against the snare of imitating the nations (the danger, Jeremiah 10:2) — the snare (following them); 'that thou enquire not after their gods... even so will I do likewise' (the curiosity and imitation forbidden, the snare of false worship, Jeremiah 10:2, the figure, the danger of dabbling with idolatry, the providence, the warning, the abominations of the nations to follow, vv.31) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: Take heed to thyself that thou be not snared by following them, after that they be destroyed from before thee; and that thou enquire not after their gods, saying, How did these nations serve their gods? even so will I do likewise
the warning not to be snared by inquiring after the nations' gods
the people warned not to be ensnared into curiosity about and imitation of the nations' worship
the snare that begins with inquiry and ends in idolatry (Jeremiah 10:2)

schools.summary: The prohibition of worshipping God after the abominations of the nations: 'thou shalt not do so unto the LORD thy God: for every abomination to the LORD, which he hateth, have they done unto their gods; for even their sons and their daughters they have burnt in the fire to their gods' — the abominations forbidden — 'thou shalt not do so unto the LORD thy God: for every abomination to the LORD, which he hateth, have they done unto their gods; for even their sons and their daughters they have burnt in the fire to their gods': God is not to be worshipped after the manner of the nations, whose worship was full of abominations, even child sacrifice (the witnesses noting the significance — the worship of God must be wholly free of the corruptions of heathen worship, the nations' worship was abominable to God, full of cruelty and filth, even the burning of their children, God's worship is not to borrow anything from it, the lesson — that God is to be worshipped as he appoints, never after the abominations he hates, the heathen worship marked by the very things God abhors, child sacrifice the height of its cruelty, 'they sacrificed their sons and their daughters unto devils,' Psalm 106:37, the worship of God to be pure, untainted by the inventions and cruelties of idolatry, the truth that man's religious inventions, far from honouring God, are abominations to him, 'in vain they do worship me,' Matthew 15:9, the figure — the purity and holiness of God's worship against the corruption of will-worship, the regulative principle that nothing of the heathen be brought into God's service, the truth that the abominations of the nations, even the most sincere and costly, are hateful to God when they are not of his appointment, the figure of the worship that must be according to God's word, not the devices of men, however zealous, the warning that the will-worship of the nations is not zeal but abomination, the figure of the purity of God's worship against the abominations of the heathen); God not to be worshipped after the nations' manner, the purity of his worship guarded (the regulative truth, Matthew 15:9) — the prohibition (not so); the nations' worship full of abominations God hates, even child sacrifice, nothing of it to be brought into God's service (the cruelty of idolatry, Psalm 106:37) — the figure (the purity of God's worship against the heathen corruption, the providence, the prohibition, the charge to add nothing and diminish nothing to follow, vv.32) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Wesley.

debates.question: Why may God not be worshipped after the manner of the nations?

sides.position: Because the nations' worship was full of abominations God hates, even the burning of their children (Psalm 106:37) - God's worship must be wholly free of the corruptions and cruelties of the heathen, the regulative truth that nothing of false worship, however sincere or costly, be brought into God's service (Matthew 15:9)

themes: Thou shalt not do so unto the LORD thy God: for every abomination to the LORD, which he hateth, have they done unto their gods; for even their sons and their daughters they have burnt in the fire to their gods
the prohibition of worshipping God after the abominations of the nations (the crux of the purity of worship against the corruptions and cruelties of the heathen)
the worship of God to be wholly free of the corruptions and cruelties of the heathen, even their child sacrifice
the regulative truth that nothing of false worship be brought into God's service (Psalm 106:37; Matthew 15:9)

schools.summary: The charge to keep God's commands whole, adding nothing and diminishing nothing: 'what thing soever I command you, observe to do it: thou shalt not add thereto, nor diminish from it' — they were to keep God's commands exactly, neither adding nor taking away (the what thing soever I command you, observe to do it, the command to keep all, the thou shalt not add thereto, nor diminish from it, the charge to add nothing and diminish nothing, the figure, the charge to keep God's word whole and entire, the rule complete, the truth that man may not alter the worship and law of God, neither adding his own inventions nor cutting off God's commands, the figure of the sufficiency and inviolability of God's word, 'ye shall not add unto the word... neither shall ye diminish ought from it,' Deuteronomy 4:2, the regulative principle restated, the worship of God to follow his command exactly, 'add thou not unto his words,' Proverbs 30:6, the warning that closes the chapter, the rule of worship complete and inviolable, against the will-worship of the nations, vv.31); the command to keep all God's word (the obedience, Deuteronomy 4:2) — the command (observe to do it); 'thou shalt not add thereto, nor diminish from it' (the word to be kept whole, neither added to nor diminished, the sufficiency of God's word, Proverbs 30:6, the figure, the inviolability of God's word, the providence, the charge, the test of the false prophet to follow, ch.13) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: What thing soever I command you, observe to do it: thou shalt not add thereto, nor diminish from it
the charge to keep God's commands whole, adding nothing and diminishing nothing
God's commands to be kept exactly, neither added to nor taken from
the rule of worship complete and inviolable against the will-worship of the nations (Deuteronomy 4:2; Proverbs 30:6)
